title: Lesson 307
transcript: logging and monitoring. This is where we're gonna start to talk in a little more detail about SIM systems or SIEM systems, security information and event management systems. Now, don't bother memorizing any of the stuff I put on the slide. I just had this cool little graphic of the evolution of a person. And I just wanted to show that there's been an evolution of these systems. They've become a lot more intelligent in the way that they're able to analyze and look for suspicious activity in the mountain of logs they were able to generate. So let's first talk about what do these SIM systems do? And I've got a really simple graphic here that kind of illustrates the power of these systems and what they're meant to do. So this little basic setup here, we've got the sketchy internet out here, we've got our little firewall and a DMZ with some bastion hosts in the DMZ, and then we've got our internal network. And a baddie pops up and starts poking away at the firewall, trying to find a way through the firewall. And this starts to generate some event data, which is forwarded from the firewall to the SIEM system. And just someone poking it away at a firewall is probably not worth looking at. It happens all the time. But now our baddie finds a way through the firewall. They find their way into say the web server. Again, not a very big deal. This is just some more event data that's sent to the SIEM system. It probably doesn't care. But then, let's say that our baddie figures out a way to compromise the web server and find a way back through our firewall into our internal network. That is probably some information, some events that we do care about. And so the same system might finally raise this to the level of showing it to an analyst on the console. And some analyst is going to have to look at this, interpret it, understand what's being shown to them. And this is a big part of what a SIM system will do is it's aggregating, collecting all this event data from all across the environment, correlating these events to look for things that in combination might be be meaningful and then show this to an analyst and do things like data enrichment, where rather than just saying IP address 172.169.12.11 has had error 157. OK, what does that actually mean? Well, that IP address might relate to this high value system. And this error message might mean this. So we can do some data enrichment to help the analyst understand exactly what the issue is that's being raised here, so that they can then make some sort of corrective action. Maybe this was a false positive and the system needs to be tuned to try and not report that again, or maybe this is a real event and someone needs to do something. It's not, my apologies, it's not an event, it's an incident, and someone needs to do something about this. So at a high level, that's what they do. And let me show you one other simple slide that highlights the power of these systems. So we've got a user here, Bob. Bob is a valid user. He has a valid Active Directory account and he decides to log into Active Directory. Because he's a valid user with a valid account, Active Directory logs him in. Active Directory logs this event occurring and replicates a copy to the SIM system. And the SIM system records this login event. Does Active Directory care about this? No, it's a valid user. Does the SIM system care about this? No, it's a valid user. Nothing of note here. Okay, now a few minutes later, Alice here comes along. Alice is a valid user with a valid SAP account and she logs into SAP. And SAP logs this event and replicates the event information to the SIEM system. Now here's the question. Does SAP care? No. Alice is a valid user. Does the SIEM system care? Potentially. Why? Well, if you see here, it's the same IP address, which looks suspicious. In most organizations, you should not be allowing shared computing. So why is Alice logging into SAP just a few minutes after Bob logged into Active Directory from the same IP address? This looks suspicious. Now, in some corporate environments, this might be fine. But maybe this is a good example of something that you might want to look at. And it's also a really good example of the power of SIM systems. Because in isolation, would Active Directory or SAP ever have identified this? No. Because in isolation, both of these events look totally fine on those two systems. But it's only when you look at these events in combination, when you correlate these events, that you see something suspicious. So this is one of the very powerful things about a SIM system. Now let's go through all the components or processes that a SIM system doesn't. This is a very high level generalized view because again, this exam does not focus on any specific technologies or systems. So we'll just talk in general terms about what SIM systems do. The first thing they do is aggregation. They're pulling in log event data from all sorts of devices across the organization. What can we pull? What can generate and provide events? pretty much everything. Our switches, our routers, our firewalls, our IDS IPS systems, our DLP systems, our servers, the operating systems, the applications on top of them, databases, data stores, the list goes on and on and on. All of this event data can be fed into our SIM system. The next step is normalization. Normalization is about cleaning up the data. So if you've got one system that's reporting the time in 12-hour clock and another system that's reporting the time in a 24-hour clock, and one is year, month, day, and another is day, year, month, or something crazy like that, it's very hard to correlate these events. So normalization is all about cleaning up the data, doing things like deduplication, so that you can then move to correlation, where you try and line these events up and see which of them might be important in combination the next step is secure storage and it doesn't actually necessarily belong at that particular spot in the in the sort of phases here can go anywhere but secure storage is about this idea that our sim system is storing all this event data that's being produced by all these devices across our network And a lot of those individual network devices, servers, things like that, especially network devices, they have much more limited storage capabilities for event data. And we might have a requirement to store event data for a long period of time. And so our SIM system can do longitudinal storage of the log data. And because we are storing event data in both the device that generated the event and the SIM system itself, it now makes it a lot more difficult for an attacker to hide their tracks. Because not only do they need to compromise say the firewall, and then wipe out their tracks on the firewall, they also have to then compromise the SIM system in trying to race their tracks there. So that's secure storage. Next is analysis. This is where we use a lot of advanced techniques to try and identify what's an event versus what's an incident. Look for that proverbial needle in the haystack and have an analyst actually look at this and decide, is this something that needs to be actioned or not? And then finally, you move on to some sort of reporting where you escalate, you document what's occurred, and maybe you even go back and tune the system to try and reduce the number of false positives and so forth. Now, where can we pull event data from? As I said, pretty much everywhere. And this is just an example. We can pull it from our DLP system, from our applications, from our servers, all sorts of places. Now, the final piece that's worth mentioning here is this concept of continuous monitoring. And you don't have to memorize the phases of continuous monitoring, but the point is this. It can be a very long, arduous process to set up a SIM system. It can take months to set these systems, maybe even longer. Once you're done setting it up, once you're done pulling in all the event data, setting up all the rules for analysis, once you've gone through all of this work, it's not like you can just kind of say you're out, I'm done, never have to do that again. No, you have to constantly, continuously be updating these systems. Because the threat environment is constantly changing. The vulnerabilities in your environment are constantly changing. The assets that you have are constantly changing. New rules need to be constantly programmed into these systems. We need to be constantly trying to reduce the false positives while not getting to the point where we're getting false negatives. So these systems need to be continuously, constantly changing. Updated. And there's one final piece that I'll mention around these systems that is of critical importance. Often there's usually way too much of a focus simply on the technology, like let's set up the connections, let's do the analysis, let's set up the system that does all of this. And what gets forgotten is the people and the process. And so I've been involved in a number of breach responses where the SIM system actually detected that the breach was occurring and even notified an analyst. And there's one event, one I can think of in particular, where the analyst was specifically notified that an event occurred and the analyst even said, whoa, this is bad, and they escalated it. and there's a major breach that all of you have heard of i can't mention the name but this is a major breach that occurred and all the technology was in place that detected the breach and the breakdown was not in the technology but in the people and the process so what's so important with these systems is not only do you have to spend all this time and money building the actual technical system You also have to have well-trained analysts that truly are able to understand what they're seeing and the right processes in place to make sure that any of these things that are occurring are getting escalated properly and actually actioned. So that's SIM systems.
